speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number eight, December, 21, 1995. The survivor's name is Ira Kaminsky. I'm Daniel [? Sedless. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This picture was taken in Chmielnik in 1938. This is my family. From left to right, standing, is my brother, Harry, who survived the war with me together. My sister, Frida, who perished together with her family. My sister, Hinda, and to the right, that was me. Sitting, from left to right, is my mother. Next to her, my sister, Hanny. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next to her is my sister, Rachel, [? Rochella. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then it's my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Below, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under [? Rochella, ?] was-- is my brother, Mendel, then the twins, Tobe and Simcha. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Three people from this picture survived. Only Harry, me, and Rachel, in the middle. The rest went to Treblinka .

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this picture saved? How do you have this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was in Israel. She went to Israel in 1933, and she wanted a picture from our family while she was, at that time, in Palestine. We took that picture and we sent it to her. And after the war, when I came to my sister in Israel, we took the picture that she has, and we made copies of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one copy, I took. I had a copy, my brother, and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is Frida's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frida's-- Frida's last name, she married to Isaac Kwashnieski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents' name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' name was Kaminsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Moshe. My mother's name was Gitl, maiden name Wasser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a picture taken before the war of my-- of my sis-- my older sister, Hannah, and my brother, Harry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was about, I would say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know exactly what year, but when Harry was a young man and Hannah was a young girl, that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Chmielnik Poland, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: We found it, also, at my sister's place in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my sister, Frida. She was older-- my other sister, Frida. Sent-- sent to Israel, also, before the war, to my sister, Hannah. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a Jewish New Year's card with a picture of my sister, Frida, sent before the war-- World War II-- to my sister, Hannah, in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken when I was in Polish school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before the war, of course. And I sent it to my sister, Hannah, before the war. And after the war, I found it in her house. In center-- I am in center. Somebody is putting around-- his hand around me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you find it? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken after the war, 1946, in Austria, Bindermichl, Austria, DP camp. To the left is my brother, Harry, my sister, Rachel, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is Rachel's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name is Broder.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this is the photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in 1946, I believe, of an-- the place was in Aus-- Bindermichl, Austria. And the-- on the picture, you see the rabbi of DP camps, American Major. And the active-- leader-- the leaders of various organizations that we had a meeting with the leadership of the Bindermichl camp and with the rabbi. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1946, in the beginning of 1946, I believe. It was-- this was a Congress of the Jewish people in Austria. And this was the dais, representatives from various organizations [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were cho-- were-- were chosen over there, in-- in the dais. The third one from the left is me, and the second from the right is Mr. Joseph Foxman, the father of Abraham Foxman, from the-- what organization? The ADL?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ADL. The father of Abe Foxman, among them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken 1947 in Cyprus. This was the central committee of the or-- the Zionist organization, Betar, and this was the central committee of the organization. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is of me, myself, taken in Israel after I was released from the-- from the Army in 1949. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken after the war, in the DP camp, Bindermichl, Austria, of my brother, Harry. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1946, and-- in Bindermichl, Austria, of my sister, Rachel, [? Rochella. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Israel in-- let's say in 194-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1950, I believe it was. And standing, from the left, is my brother-in-law, Mordechai Broder. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was in a concentration camp, and with-- together in-- in the-- in the group of Schindler. And he was one of the people who was saved by Schindler. I-- I met Schindler through him when we were traveling in 194-- the beginning of 1947, in Germany. I met Schindler. And whenever Schindler was in Israel, he was over there with my brother-and-law and my sister, Rachel. Next it's the pic-- next, I am. And to the right is my brother-in-law, Shlomo Naiman, the husband of my sister, Hannah. Next is my sister, Hannah. Then from the left, second line, is my sister, Rachel. Then her daughter-- her daughter, [INAUDIBLE], when she was a baby. And then [INAUDIBLE], the daughter of my sister, Hannah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please. This is the photograph of--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is-- this is my wedding picture with my wife, Tillie. Her maiden name was Rosenzweig. And me, at my wedding. It was 1955 in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is-- was taken in 1964 from our organization from our-- these are people from-- in the United States from our city, Chmielnik. And this was the committee from the [NON-ENGLISH] organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is from me, to-- from the left, my-- my son, my oldest son, Marvin. And then my son, Howard, and my brother, Harry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my wife, Tillie-- Tillie Kaminsky. Her maiden name was Rosenzweig. She was born in Brooklyn. And the next to her is my son, Rabbi Howard Kaminsky. And next it's me, myself. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to introduce my wife, to my right, Tillie Kaminsky, and my son, standing behind me, Rabbi Howard Kaminsky. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Howard?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to take an opportunity. I was very, very blessed to have two exceptionally loving parents. My mother was always showering me with love and concern, and my father has always been one of the best friends I've ever had in my life. He's-- he gave me so much in life. He gave me love, security as a child. He instilled in me and my brother an exceptional love for Judaism, and he's an all-around great guy. I've got two great parents over here, and I just want my great-great-great grandkids to know, you had-- you were very blessed to have some very special people you came from. And as far as my brother goes, he wishes that he could be here. And let me express-- express the sentiment for him that he's also-- he's very proud to have these two people as his-- as his parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
